metadata.title: Königsberg Bridges — Euler's Theorem
metadata.note: CLI video: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06–B08 slates · B09 outro

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: In 1736 the citizens of Königsberg asked: can you walk across all seven bridges exactly once and return to your start? Euler didn't just answer the question — he invented the machinery to answer it for any map, ever. That machinery is graph theory, and it runs in a twelve-line script.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'The 1736 puzzle that invented graph theory — can you cross all 7 bridges exactly once?'; no terminal yet

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude to build the solver. It accepts a graph as an adjacency dict. Compute the degree of each vertex. Print a degree table. Verdict: Euler circuit exists if all degrees are even, Euler path exists if exactly two are odd, otherwise no Euler path or circuit. If an Euler circuit exists, find it with Hierholzer's algorithm. Test on the Königsberg graph and a simple cycle.

props.command: claude "Write a Python script: accepts graph as adjacency dict. Compute degree of each vertex. Print degree table. Verdict: 'Euler circuit exists' (all even), 'Euler path exists' (exactly 2 odd), or 'No Euler path or circuit'. If circuit exists, find it with Hierholzer's. Test: Königsberg {'N':['C','C','W'],'C':['N','N','S','S','W'],'S':['C','C','W'],'W':['N','C','S']} and simple cycle {'X':['Y','Z'],'Y':['X','Z'],'Z':['X','Y']}."

beats.narration_text: Read the Euler condition — odd underscore vertices equals the list of vertices v where the degree of v modulo two is not zero. If that list has exactly two entries, a path exists. If it's empty, a circuit exists. One predicate, two hundred and eighty-eight years of certainty.

Draw it. Four nodes — North, South, West, Center. Seven edges. The degree labels appear in red on every node: three, five, three, three. All odd. The verdict: no Euler path or circuit. Euler knew this without drawing it — we can see it because he gave us the rule.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: Königsberg graph with 4 nodes and 7 edges (multi-edges offset); crimson degree labels on all nodes; verdict banner confirming no Euler path

beats.narration_text: Now test Euler's fix. If you add one bridge — one edge connecting North to South — two nodes go from odd to even. Iterate: update the solver to test the modified Königsberg graph with one added edge N-S, and report whether an Euler circuit now exists.

props.command: claude "update euler_solver.py: test modified Königsberg with one added edge N-S. New graph: N has 4 edges (C,C,W,S), S has 4 edges (C,C,W,N), W=3, C=5. Print degree table and verdict. Then find the Euler path/circuit if it exists."

beats.role_note: slate — fill with screen recording or Manim
beats.narration_text: Add one bridge. North goes from degree three to four — even. South goes from three to four — even. West stays three — odd. Center stays five — odd. Two odd nodes remain: an Euler path exists, not a circuit. Add a second bridge between West and Center and all degrees flip to even — circuit achieved.
beats.visual_intent: Degree table for modified graph showing N=4, S=4, W=3, C=5 → Euler path exists; second modification showing full circuit

beats.narration_text: Euler's theorem reduces an impossible-sounding puzzle to a single count: how many vertices have odd degree? Zero means walk it as a circuit. Two means walk it as a path. More than two means you can't walk it at all — and no amount of creativity changes that.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: three cases — '0 odd: circuit · 2 odd: path · 4 odd: impossible'; Königsberg as the canonical example

beats.narration_text: Your move: map a real logistics problem — a delivery route, a circuit board trace — as a graph and run the Euler check. If more than two nodes have odd degree, find the minimum number of edges to add to make it traversable. That's the Chinese Postman Problem.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) model delivery route as adjacency dict, 2) run euler_check, 3) find min edges to add for Euler path

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
